steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to make a generalization from the study's findings — a broad conclusion about parks and happiness overall, not a description of method, sample size, or a comparison of two specific cities.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Geotagged social media posts were analyzed for expressed happiness in both San Diego and Boston.: describes only the methodology and scope, not a finding about parks and happiness. ✗
  • Choice B — Though it can vary from city to city, the overall effect of parks on happiness is a positive one.: draws a broad conclusion from two data points: the average benefit of 0.10 (above zero — positive) and city-to-city variation in that benefit. This is a true generalization from the findings. ✓
  • Choice C — San Diego's parks had a positive effect on happiness, but this was not true of Boston's parks, which had a below-average effect.: misrepresents the data — Boston's benefit was greater than 0, meaning it was still positive, even though it was below the average benefit of 0.10. ✗
  • Choice D — By comparing the happiness scores of posts made in parks and posts made elsewhere, the study determined the effect of parks on happiness.: describes the study's method, not a generalized finding about what parks do to happiness. ✗
That matches Choice B — Though it can vary from city to city, the overall effect of parks on happiness is a positive one..
